title: Ensure That Service Account Has No Admin Privileges

input_row.title: Ensure That Service Account Has No Admin Privileges
input_row.description: A service account is a special Google account that belongs to an application or a VM, instead of to an individual end-user. The application uses the service account to call the service's Google API so that users aren't directly involved. It's recommended not to use admin access for ServiceAccount.
input_row.rationale: Service accounts represent service-level security of the Resources (application or a VM) which can be determined by the roles assigned to it. Enrolling ServiceAccount with Admin rights gives full access to an assigned application or a VM. A ServiceAccount Access holder can perform critical actions like delete, update change settings, etc. without user intervention. For this reason, it's recommended that service accounts not have Admin rights.
input_row.audit: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to IAM & admin/IAM using https://console.cloud.google.com/iam- admin/iam 2. Under the IAM Tab look for VIEW BY PRINCIPALS 3. Filter PRINCIPALS using type : Service account 4. Look for the Service Account with the nomenclature: SERVICE_ACCOUNT_NAME@PROJECT_ID.iam.gserviceaccount.com 5. Ensure that there are no such Service Accounts with roles containing *Admin or *admin or role matching Editor or role matching Owner under Role column. From Google Cloud CLI 1. Get the policy that you want to modify, and write it to a JSON file: gcloud projects get-iam-policy PROJECT_ID --format json > iam.json 2. The contents of the JSON file will look similar to the following. Note that role of members group associated with each serviceaccount does not contain *Admin or *admin or does not match roles/editor or does not match roles/owner. This recommendation is only applicable to User-Managed user-created service accounts. These accounts have the nomenclature: SERVICE_ACCOUNT_NAME@PROJECT_ID.iam.gserviceaccount.com. Note that some Google-managed, Google-created service accounts have the same naming format, and should be excluded (e.g., appsdev-apps-dev-script- auth@system.gserviceaccount.com which needs the Owner role). Sample Json output: { "bindings": [ { "members": [ "serviceAccount:our-project-123@appspot.gserviceaccount.com", ], "role": "roles/appengine.appAdmin" }, { "members": [ "user:email1@gmail.com" ], "role": "roles/owner" }, { "members": [ "serviceAccount:our-project-123@appspot.gserviceaccount.com", "serviceAccount:123456789012-compute@developer.gserviceaccount.com" ], "role": "roles/editor" } ], "etag": "BwUjMhCsNvY=", "version": 1 }
input_row.remediation: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to IAM & admin/IAM using https://console.cloud.google.com/iam- admin/iam 2. Under the IAM Tab look for VIEW BY PRINCIPALS 3. Filter PRINCIPALS using type : Service account 4. Look for the Service Account with the Principal nomenclature: SERVICE_ACCOUNT_NAME@PROJECT_ID.iam.gserviceaccount.com 5. Identify User-Managed user created service account with roles containing *Admin or *admin or role matching Editor or role matching Owner under Role Column. 6. Click on Edit (Pencil Icon) for the Service Account, it will open all the roles which are assigned to the Service Account. 7. Click the Delete bin icon to remove the role from the Principal (service account in this case) From Google Cloud CLI gcloud projects get-iam-policy PROJECT_ID --format json > iam.json 1. Using a text editor, Remove Role which contains roles/*Admin or roles/*admin or matched roles/editor or matches 'roles/owner`. Add a role to the bindings array that defines the group members and the role for those members. For example, to grant the role roles/appengine.appViewer to the ServiceAccount which is roles/editor, you would change the example shown below as follows: { "bindings": [ { "members": [ "serviceAccount:our-project-123@appspot.gserviceaccount.com", ], "role": "roles/appengine.appViewer" }, { "members": [ "user:email1@gmail.com" ], "role": "roles/owner" }, { "members": [ "serviceAccount:our-project-123@appspot.gserviceaccount.com", "serviceAccount:123456789012-compute@developer.gserviceaccount.com" ], "role": "roles/editor" } ], "etag": "BwUjMhCsNvY=" } 2. Update the project's IAM policy: gcloud projects set-iam-policy PROJECT_ID iam.json
input_row.impact: Removing *Admin or *admin or Editor or Owner role assignments from service accounts may break functionality that uses impacted service accounts. Required role(s) should be assigned to impacted service accounts in order to restore broken functionalities.
input_row.default_value: User Managed (and not user-created) default service accounts have the Editor (roles/editor) role assigned to them to support GCP services they offer. By default, there are no roles assigned to User Managed User created service accounts.
input_row.additional_information: Default (user-managed but not user-created) service accounts have the Editor (roles/editor) role assigned to them to support GCP services they offer. Such Service accounts are: PROJECT_NUMBER-compute@developer.gserviceaccount.com, PROJECT_ID@appspot.gserviceaccount.com.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_iam_service_account_admin_privileges_check

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
1. **GCP API calls or Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) SDK methods to use:**
   - Use the `gcloud` command: `gcloud projects get-iam-policy PROJECT_ID --format json` to retrieve the IAM policy for the specified project.

2. **GCP CLI commands (if applicable):**
   - `gcloud projects get-iam-policy PROJECT_ID --format json` 

3. **Specific resources to enumerate:**
   - User-managed service accounts identified by the naming convention `SERVICE_ACCOUNT_NAME@PROJECT_ID.iam.gserviceaccount.com`.

4. **Exact configuration properties/settings to validate:**
   - Check the roles assigned to each service account to ensure they do not include any roles with the suffixes `*Admin`, `*admin`, or match `roles/editor`, or `roles/owner`.

5. **Clear Pass/Fail criteria with expected values:**
   - **Pass:** No user-managed service accounts should have roles containing `*Admin`, `*admin`, or matching `roles/editor` or `roles/owner`.
   - **Fail:** If any user-managed service account is found with these roles assigned.

6. **Example:**
   - Execute `gcloud projects get-iam-policy YOUR_PROJECT_ID --format json | grep -i 'serviceAccount:'` to list service accounts and their roles.
   - Parse the output to check for any of the forbidden role assignments mentioned above.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE

- The classification of the audit approach as "Automated" is correct. This control can be effectively checked using GCP APIs and the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) command-line tool.
- The use of `gcloud projects get-iam-policy PROJECT_ID --format json` is appropriate for retrieving IAM policies, which can then be parsed to check for admin privileges assigned to service accounts.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 8

- The automation details are mostly accurate. The `gcloud` command specified is valid and correctly retrieves the IAM policy for a project.
- The method of checking for roles with suffixes `*Admin`, `*admin`, or matching `roles/editor`, or `roles/owner` is a sound approach to identify admin privileges.
- The program name `gcp_iam_service_account_admin_privileges_check` is descriptive but could be more specific to indicate the nature of the check, such as `gcp_iam_service_account_role_check`.
- The example command provided for listing service accounts and their roles is useful, but the use of `grep` is not ideal for JSON parsing. A more robust approach would involve using `jq` or similar tools to parse JSON output.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- Consider using `jq` for parsing JSON output instead of `grep`, which is not reliable for structured data. For example:
  ```bash
  gcloud projects get-iam-policy YOUR_PROJECT_ID --format=json | jq '.bindings[] | select(.members[] | test("serviceAccount:")) | select(.role | test("Admin$|admin$|roles/editor|roles/owner"))'
  ```
- The program name could be improved for clarity and specificity. Suggested name: `gcp_iam_service_account_admin_role_check`.
- Ensure that the script or program checks for all potential variations of admin roles, including custom roles that might have admin-like privileges.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

- The assessment is well-structured and uses appropriate GCP tools and methods to automate the compliance check. The technical details are mostly accurate, and the approach is sound.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**

- Modify: Update the example command to use `jq` for JSON parsing instead of `grep` to ensure accurate and reliable data extraction.
- Consider renaming the program to `gcp_iam_service_account_admin_role_check` for better clarity and specificity in describing the security check being performed.
